title: Are AI trading tools actually useful in real trading?
author: Peaceaceous-443
selftext:
My brother trades manually, and from what I’ve seen, he relies mostly on experience, charts, and understanding the market rather than using any automated tools.

That made me think about all these trading bots people talk about. They claim to handle analysis and decisions automatically, but I’m not sure how that holds up when the market gets unpredictable.

So, I'II like to know, do trading bots actually help in real trading, or do most people still prefer manual trading?

comments.author: UDPSendToFailed
comments.body: I think trading bots aren't good for much. AI's advantage is being able to process information you throw at it and break it down for you to use and understand, like quarterly reports, specific sector dynamics, and so on. For that, I've been using AI for about a year already, and it's been pretty helpful, I made roughly 29% profits.

replies.author: FAUST_VII
replies.body:
Whats your workflow / how do you prompt / what ai u use?

I have gpt pro (for coding) and it wasn't really helpful at all with stocks. If I followed any of its recommendations, I wouldn't have invested in anything else but world

comments.author: StevenVinyl
comments.body: that's why you need a non-deterministic bot, aka agent. It reasons through your prompt and can switch based on market conditions. I'm using a platform called Cod3x to launch these agents and have a few running already.

replies.author: Remko76
replies.body: And how are these agents doing so far?

replies.author: StevenVinyl
replies.body: depending on how good your strategy is, most are doing great, some are a bit underwater. It's still a skill based game, it's not a silver bullet.

comments.author: BuildwithPublic
comments.body: Both can work — the real question is whether your automation is built on infrastructure that can actually execute. The interesting shift happening now is brokerages like u/public opening up direct API and MCP/CLI access, so your strategy logic connects straight to order execution. That's when automation starts to actually complement the manual instincts your brother has built up.

comments.author: Denis_Kurilchik
comments.body:
Bots help when the edge is explicit and the rules are mechanical. When it involves judgment or context-switching, they are brain dead.

Opacity is frequently overlooked. Most retail bots either backtest well and fail live, or perform inconsistently, and you cant tell which is happening until you've already lost money. Your brother's approach, whatever his edge is, is at least visible to him. That's harder to replicate in an automated system than most people expect.

Where AI actually adds value in trading is processing information at scale: earnings calls, sector dynamics, order flow. Faster than a human and more consistent. 

Gut feeling sometimes has part for your brother too.

comments.author: ReceptionUnlucky9455
comments.body: Asking AI when to trade or predict market is not a wise move, if you want test 100 same trades and check. But you can automate workflow and then use AI to refine your strategy is possible. It’s a time consuming process like any other skill though.

comments.author: Realistic-Garlic-508
comments.body: I am always more comfortable having the control on my side when it comes to finances, whether that be making or losing money, at least knowing that decisions were your own and no one else to be blamed, having said that, with my background primarily being in data and analytics and the many spreadsheet based logics and programming that I used in the past for analysis, decided to take those learnings across years into building out a  platform that makes the analysis and decisions consistent, data driven and user friendly. It is a hybrid between real statistical data (history) and use of AI for opinion and suggestions. Currently backtesting it before opening it up, but have setup a preview signup for folks who are interested to try it out.

comments.author: Chemical_Badger6227
comments.body:
We tested this rigorously. Built 34 AI/ML strategies for crypto over a month. The best showed a Sharpe of 4.50 in backtesting. Then we found the bugs. A one-line timing error was letting the AI see current prices before trading. Fix that, and the edge disappeared. 

We stripped out the AI entirely and replaced it with a 4-line rule: buy when volatility is expanding and the trend is up. No ML, no training. The simple rule: Sharpe 1.4 on ETH. The AI: Sharpe 0.2.  

AI is useful for processing information (earnings, on-chain data, sentiment). It's not useful for predicting prices — at least not at the retail level with hourly data. Your brother's manual approach probably has more edge than most bots, because he can adapt to context that a model can't see.
